title: Darwin's Paradox!
anchor_summary: Cinematic single-player platform-adventure focused on traversal, stealthy evasion, and environmental puzzle-solving in a hostile industrial setting, with a distinctive nonhuman moveset driving progression.

similar_games.title: INSIDE
similar_games.why_similar: Cinematic puzzle-platforming through a sinister industrial world built around trap avoidance, environmental hazards, and authored progression.

similar_games.title: Little Nightmares II
similar_games.why_similar: Linear suspenseful platform-adventure mixing stealth, platforming, and environmental puzzles while escaping oversized threats.

similar_games.title: Oddworld: New 'n' Tasty
similar_games.why_similar: Escape-driven cinematic platformer with stealth, trap navigation, hostile facilities, and puzzle-led movement through dangerous industrial spaces.

similar_games.title: Planet of Lana
similar_games.why_similar: Storybook cinematic adventure emphasizing traversal, companion/environment puzzles, and a smooth narrative-driven campaign over combat.

similar_games.title: Stray
similar_games.why_similar: Animal-protagonist adventure centered on bespoke movement abilities, exploration, light platforming, stealthy moments, and environmental problem-solving in a mysterious machine world.

payload.anchor_summary: Cinematic single-player platform-adventure focused on traversal, stealthy evasion, and environmental puzzle-solving in a hostile industrial setting, with a distinctive nonhuman moveset driving progression.

llm_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored store descriptions consistently frame Darwin's Paradox! as a single-player, story-driven 3D platformer-adventure with puzzle solving and light stealth. The strongest recurring gameplay signals are 'platforming sequences,' 'moments of puzzle-solving,' and verbs like swim, wall-climb, hide, infiltrate, jump, camouflage, and shoot ink to overcome traps and escape. The presentation is described as a cinematic animated-movie-like adventure in a mysterious industrial complex with sprawling handcrafted levels, which supports a linear/level-based campaign structure and a secondary cinematic action-adventure flavor. Combat is not presented as dominant; instead, stealthy avoidance, traversal, and puzzle progression appear to define the core loop, making action_platformer the best fit.

resolved_similar_games.why_similar: Cinematic puzzle-platforming through a sinister industrial world built around trap avoidance, environmental hazards, and authored progression.
Linear suspenseful platform-adventure mixing stealth, platforming, and environmental puzzles while escaping oversized threats.
Escape-driven cinematic platformer with stealth, trap navigation, hostile facilities, and puzzle-led movement through dangerous industrial spaces.
Storybook cinematic adventure emphasizing traversal, companion/environment puzzles, and a smooth narrative-driven campaign over combat.
Animal-protagonist adventure centered on bespoke movement abilities, exploration, light platforming, stealthy moments, and environmental problem-solving in a mysterious machine world.

title: Meow Moments: Celebrating Myth & Machine
anchor_summary: A cozy, family-friendly hidden-object game built around scanning detailed illustrated scenes to find many cats/characters and interact with light balloon-popping elements, very much in the relaxing hidden-cats picture-puzzle lane.

similar_games.title: A Castle Full of Cats
similar_games.why_similar: Relaxed hidden-object gameplay focused on finding cats across dense illustrated scenes with a cozy, low-pressure structure.

similar_games.title: An Arcade Full of Cats
similar_games.why_similar: Another cat-centered hidden-object game built around searching busy hand-drawn environments for large numbers of cleverly placed felines.

similar_games.title: Hidden Cats in Paris
similar_games.why_similar: Very close match in casual hidden-cats scene scanning, with charming artwork and a soothing, accessible play loop.

similar_games.title: Cats Hidden in Italy
similar_games.why_similar: Shares the minimalist hidden-cats formula of carefully inspecting themed environments to uncover concealed cats.

similar_games.title: 100 Hidden Cats
similar_games.why_similar: Similar pure hidden-object structure centered on spotting many hidden cats in static illustrated scenes for relaxed progression.

llm_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Multiple stored descriptions consistently identify the game as a cozy hidden-object adventure focused on finding cats, characters, and balloons across two illustrated scenes/settings. The copy emphasizes pressure-free, family-friendly search gameplay, detailed static environments, intuitive controls, and solo play, with no indication of combat, platforming, racing, or broader action-adventure systems. Metacritic also labels the genre as Hidden Object. This strongly supports hidden_object_puzzle as the core gameplay identity.

However, the allowed taxonomy archetype list does not contain a clear fit for a first-person cooking or restaurant-work simulation. Available simulation-adjacent archetypes such as management_tycoon, life_sim, farming_sim, engineering_sandbox_sim, or transport_sim would misrepresent the game's primary hands-on culinary simulation loop.

title: Last Man Sitting
anchor_summary: Co-op-friendly roguelite horde shooter built around surviving escalating enemy waves, leveling up mid-run, drafting power-ups for synergies, and mixing gunplay with some melee in fast arcade-style arenas.

similar_games.title: Risk of Rain 2
similar_games.why_similar: Run-based co-op horde shooter with escalating waves, constant movement, mid-run item builds, and stacking synergies.

similar_games.title: Crab Champions
similar_games.why_similar: Arcade-y third-person roguelite shooter focused on arena combat, mobility, co-op play, and draftable upgrades between fights.

similar_games.title: Roboquest
similar_games.why_similar: Fast roguelite shooter with build drafting, weapon/loadout variation, and skill-driven runs, though more FPS and less wave-arena focused.

similar_games.title: Gunfire Reborn
similar_games.why_similar: Co-op roguelite shooter with repeated runs, character/loadout differentiation, and heavy emphasis on upgrade combinations and progression.

similar_games.title: KILL KNIGHT
similar_games.why_similar: High-intensity arena survival with bullet-hell pressure and score-chasing combat, though more twin-stick and less co-op roguelite shooter.

payload.anchor_summary: Co-op-friendly roguelite horde shooter built around surviving escalating enemy waves, leveling up mid-run, drafting power-ups for synergies, and mixing gunplay with some melee in fast arcade-style arenas.

llm_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently identify the game as a roguelite action shooter built around surviving progressively harder waves, leveling up during runs, drafting power-ups, unlocking chair upgrades, and finding build synergies. The core loop is presented as single-player or up to 4-player co-op, with PvP arena/king-of-the-hill described as an extra mode rather than the main identity. Combat is dominant, fast, and hybridized between shooting and hacking/slashing, with minibosses and bosses closing out wave progression. This strongly fits co_op_action_roguelite over arena_fps because the text emphasizes roguelite run structure, power-up drafting, co-op survival, and meta-upgrades rather than primarily match-based FPS competition.

resolved_similar_games.why_similar: Run-based co-op horde shooter with escalating waves, constant movement, mid-run item builds, and stacking synergies.
Arcade-y third-person roguelite shooter focused on arena combat, mobility, co-op play, and draftable upgrades between fights.
Fast roguelite shooter with build drafting, weapon/loadout variation, and skill-driven runs, though more FPS and less wave-arena focused.
Co-op roguelite shooter with repeated runs, character/loadout differentiation, and heavy emphasis on upgrade combinations and progression.
High-intensity arena survival with bullet-hell pressure and score-chasing combat, though more twin-stick and less co-op roguelite shooter.

title: Subliminal
anchor_summary: First-person psychological horror centered on exploration through uncanny liminal spaces, with environment-driven progression via light and perspective manipulation, plus chase-pressure rather than combat-heavy encounters.

similar_games.title: Superliminal
similar_games.why_similar: First-person puzzle game built around forced perspective and spatial reality-bending, making it the clearest mechanical comparison for Subliminal’s perspective-driven puzzles.

similar_games.title: Layers of Fear
similar_games.why_similar: Psychological first-person horror focused on warped interiors, environmental instability, and progressing through surreal spaces with minimal combat.

similar_games.title: Viewfinder
similar_games.why_similar: Reality-altering first-person puzzle design where changing visual perspective reshapes traversable space, overlapping strongly with Subliminal’s environment manipulation.

similar_games.title: The Complex: Found Footage
similar_games.why_similar: A recognizable Backrooms-style horror comp built around navigating liminal spaces, unease, and pursuit through uncanny environments rather than combat systems.

similar_games.title: Madison
similar_games.why_similar: First-person psychological horror emphasizing puzzle-solving under tension, oppressive atmosphere, and frightening encounters triggered by exploration and hesitation.

llm_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently frame Subliminal as a first-person psychological horror experience centered on exploring handcrafted open-ended memory-spaces and solving light/perspective-based environmental puzzles. The Steam detailed description emphasizes hallucination-like instability, unsettling spaces, a pursuing inhuman figure, and psychologically terrifying encounters, which strongly supports psychological_horror over action_horror or survival_horror. Gameplay identity appears to be exploration plus puzzle-solving under threat rather than combat-driven play; chase/pressure sequences justify light survival-oriented encounter framing, but not dominant combat. Metacritic explicitly labels it 'First-Person Adventure,' while Steam copy repeatedly stresses psychological horror, environmental puzzle solving, and single-player progression through authored spaces.

resolved_similar_games.why_similar: First-person puzzle game built around forced perspective and spatial reality-bending, making it the clearest mechanical comparison for Subliminal’s perspective-driven puzzles.
Psychological first-person horror focused on warped interiors, environmental instability, and progressing through surreal spaces with minimal combat.

First-person psychological horror emphasizing puzzle-solving under tension, oppressive atmosphere, and frightening encounters triggered by exploration and hesitation.

title: Raccoin: Coin Pusher Roguelike
anchor_summary: A run-based combo game that fuses arcade coin-pusher physics with roguelike/deckbuilder-style build crafting, where special coins, items, and character kits create escalating synergies over short replayable sessions.

similar_games.title: Luck be a Landlord
similar_games.why_similar: Another roguelite built around a gambling-machine format, with symbol combo stacking and run-defining synergies.

similar_games.title: Balatro
similar_games.why_similar: Shares the dopamine-heavy roguelike deckbuilder loop of assembling escalating score combos from item and ruleset interactions.

similar_games.title: Roundguard
similar_games.why_similar: Arcade physics play is blended with roguelike progression, making moment-to-moment shots and build choices both central.

similar_games.title: Coin Crypt
similar_games.why_similar: A coin-themed roguelike/deckbuilding hybrid where currency-like pieces and tactical synergies drive each run.

payload.anchor_summary: A run-based combo game that fuses arcade coin-pusher physics with roguelike/deckbuilder-style build crafting, where special coins, items, and character kits create escalating synergies over short replayable sessions.

llm_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored Steam copy gives strong gameplay evidence: this is an arcade-style coin pusher built around repeated runs, score-chasing, and physics-driven coin drops, with roguelike deckbuilding layered on top through collectible special coins, 150 items, character-specific loadouts/playstyles, endless mode, and multiple difficulty levels. It is not literal pinball, but within the available taxonomy the closest fit is pinball because the core interaction is a non-combat arcade table/machine focused on chaining effects, manipulating a physical playfield, and maximizing payouts/scores. Secondary card_battler reflects the explicit 'roguelike deckbuilder' structure, though card play itself is absent, so it remains secondary rather than primary.

resolved_similar_games.why_similar: Physics-driven pachinko turns are wrapped in a roguelike run structure with relic synergies and build planning.
Another roguelite built around a gambling-machine format, with symbol combo stacking and run-defining synergies.

The strongest gameplay identity is therefore soulslike_action_rpg rather than metroidvania: the game is explicitly top-down rather than side-scrolling, and the copy emphasizes boss-centric challenging combat, build/loadout variety, and soulslike structure more than platforming. Metroidvania remains a strong secondary influence because the store text explicitly cites 'Metroidvania elements' and an interconnected world with secret optional areas.

llm_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored DB evidence is sufficient to accept this as a visual novel. Metacritic explicitly labels the game's genre as 'Visual Novel,' which is strong direct evidence. The descriptive copy is entirely story- and character-focused, emphasizing Nobushige Sanada, the Ten Braves, their bonds, and a historical Sengoku-era narrative aimed at otome players. No concrete evidence supports action, strategy, simulation, or combat-driven play, and the current audit already flags combat_presence as none plus authored_linear and historical signals. Together this supports a single-player, non-combat, historically themed visual novel identity.

title: The Posthumous Investigation
anchor_summary: A single-player time-loop murder mystery built around repeating one day, learning suspect schedules, manipulating character routines, and using deduction tools to reconstruct a crime and accuse the culprit.

similar_games.title: Paradise Killer
similar_games.why_similar: Open-ended detective structure with suspect interviews, evidence gathering, timeline reconstruction, and making your own accusation.

similar_games.title: Return of the Obra Dinn
similar_games.why_similar: Logic-heavy investigation centered on linking clues, reconstructing events, and identifying the truth through deduction rather than combat.

similar_games.title: The Case of the Golden Idol
similar_games.why_similar: Pure deduction mystery that asks you to study scenes, connect evidence, and infer what happened with minimal hand-holding.

llm_taxonomy.evidence_summary: The stored descriptions provide solid high-level identity: a single-player hand-drawn noir time-loop murder mystery set in 1937 Rio, centered on learning character schedules, linking evidence on a Thinking Board, reconstructing a timeline, manipulating routines, and solving the case through logic. However, the evidence does not cleanly map to any allowed Similar Games Taxonomy V2 archetype. It strongly suggests investigative adventure / detective puzzle design, but there is no explicit support for the required loops of hidden_object_puzzle (hidden-object scenes or point-and-click object hunt), visual_novel, immersive_sim_action, or other available archetypes. Because the gameplay identity is clear yet sits outside the current archetype list, classification should be rejected rather than forced into a mismatched bucket.

llm_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored evidence clearly describes a single-player, procedurally generated run-based top-down shooter/roguelike with a distinctive hybrid structure: turn-based tactical planning blended with real-time bullet-hell action, plus build synergies, unlockable characters, weapons, and missions. However, the allowed Similar Games Taxonomy V2 archetype list does not contain a good-fit archetype for a single-player tactical/bullet-hell roguelike shooter of this kind. Available shooter archetypes such as arena_fps, military_fps, tactical_shooter, hero_shooter, loot_shooter, or extraction_shooter do not match the top-down run-based roguelike identity, and turn_based_tactics would also be misleading because the game is explicitly a shooter with hybrid real-time bullet-hell combat rather than primarily overhead squad tactics. Because the gameplay evidence is strong but does not map cleanly to an allowed archetype, rejection is the safest taxonomy outcome.

resolved_similar_games.why_similar: Shares the core time-moves-when-you-do combat hook, emphasizing deliberate movement and projectile dodging over constant real-time action.

llm_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently and explicitly define the game as a tough-as-nails 3D platformer centered on 'straight forward twitch reflex platforming' and 'precision platforming action.' The text emphasizes brutal but fair levels, repeated deaths from hazards, wall-leaping, and escalating difficulty, which strongly matches precision_platformer. Metacritic genre labeling supports third-person 3D platforming, while the copy's references to 'a bunch of levels,' Dark World levels, and boss fights support a level-based campaign structure with boss-centric encounters. Combat is not described as a primary loop, so the game fits precision platformer far better than action_platformer or 3d_collectathon; unlockable secrets are present, but collection is not framed as the core objective.

llm_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently present Island of Hearts as a fully acted first-person interactive romance centered on dialogue choices, branching outcomes, hidden storylines, and multiple endings. The dominant loop is narrative progression through choice-making and relationship outcomes rather than combat, traversal, or systemic RPG play. 'Clues, exploration, and mini games' are described as supplemental, not identity-defining. Single-player campaign structure, authored branching narrative, interpersonal romance drama, casual input demands, and non-combat gameplay strongly fit visual_novel.

title: MARVEL MaXimum Collection
anchor_summary: A retro compilation/archive product: a multi-game collection of classic Marvel titles across arcade and home-console versions, emphasizing preservation features like online play for select games, save states, rewind, music/art galleries, and a curated historical package rather than one unified core game.

llm_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently frame MARVEL MaXimum Collection as a retro compilation/archive containing multiple distinct legacy Marvel games and versions across arcade, 8-bit, 16-bit, and portable platforms. The text explicitly says it is 'spanning multiple genres' and then lists materially different included games and modes: beat-'em-up action (X-Men: The Arcade Game), a platforming variant of Captain America and The Avengers, co-op side-scrolling action in Maximum Carnage/Separation Anxiety, and an infamously difficult shooter in Silver Surfer. Because the package aggregates several different gameplay identities rather than presenting one dominant, unified core loop, there is not a single taxonomy archetype that can be supported strongly enough. Mode labels like co-op, multiplayer, and single-player are also compilation-wide and do not resolve the gameplay identity problem.

title: Dying Light: The Beast Restored Land
anchor_summary: Enhanced/complete-edition version of Dying Light: The Beast: an open-world first-person zombie survival action game built around parkour traversal, weighty melee combat, day/night pressure, scavenging/crafting, and optional co-op, with Restored Land adding harsher persistence and survival consequences.

similar_games.title: Dying Light: The Beast
similar_games.why_similar: Restored Land is the enhanced package of the base game, preserving the same core open-world first-person parkour, melee survival, and co-op structure.

similar_games.title: Dying Light 2 Stay Human
similar_games.why_similar: Same first-person zombie action formula with freerunning traversal, scavenging, crafting, day/night risk shifts, and co-op in an open world.

similar_games.title: Dying Light
similar_games.why_similar: Direct franchise predecessor centered on first-person parkour, brutal melee combat, looting, and the signature night-time escalation loop.

similar_games.title: Dead Island 2
similar_games.why_similar: Shares first-person zombie-slaying with emphasis on visceral melee weapons, exploration, looting, quests, and co-op, though with less parkour focus.

similar_games.title: State of Decay 2
similar_games.why_similar: Another zombie survival game focused on scavenging, resource pressure, dangerous night outings, and persistent consequences, though with a broader management layer.

llm_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored official descriptions consistently identify this as a long-form open-world Dying Light campaign focused on exploration of a large handcrafted region, first-person melee combat against infected, grounded parkour traversal, scavenging/loot, day-night survival pressure, authored story progression, and optional shared-progression co-op. Restored Land is described as an enhanced version of the same core game with harsher survival rules, permanent world consequences, and one-life options, which intensify survival elements without changing the underlying gameplay identity. That supports open_world_action_adventure as the primary archetype, with strong action_horror and exploration_survival_adventure traits.

resolved_similar_games.why_similar: Restored Land is the enhanced package of the base game, preserving the same core open-world first-person parkour, melee survival, and co-op structure.
Same first-person zombie action formula with freerunning traversal, scavenging, crafting, day/night risk shifts, and co-op in an open world.
